coredata.dc:title: Oxidation-induced quenching mechanism of ultrabright red carbon dots and application in antioxidant RCDs/PVA film
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Dongna
Kou, Erfeng
Li, Wei
Zhang, Haoran
Zhang, Xuejie
Zhuang, Jianle
Liu, Yingliang
Hu, Chaofan
Zheng, Yinjian
Yang, Qichang
Lei, Bingfu
coredata.dc:description: Red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) dominated by surface state emission mechanism were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method, the photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of RCDs reaches to 78.3% in N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF). RCDs mainly absorb UV and yellowish green light and emit red light in the region from 550 to 800 nm, which perfectly matches the light needed by plant for photosynthesis. Besides, the fluorescence quenching of RCDs is ascribed to oxidation on the surface of RCDs by oxygen. Further evidences about that are obtained from the changes in fluorescence, singlet oxygen (1O2) signal of RCDs and the amount of O elements between the RCDs and quenched RCDs (QCDs). Antioxidant RCDs/PVA film, served as an agricultural sunlight conversion film, consisting of RCDs, polyvinyl alcohol-1799 (PVA-1799) and ascorbic acid, not only converts yellowish green light (500–610 nm) to red light (610–800 nm) from RCDs with tough, environment-friendly, waterproof properties from PVA-1799, but also gain antioxidant ability on account of ascorbic acid preventing the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film from fluorescence quenching. When antioxidant RCDs/PVA film was applied to the practical agricultural planting, the fresh weight, dry weight and the chlorophyll a content of the mung bean sprouts increased by 10.4% and 13.9%, and 7.1%, respectively. The application of the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film improve the utilization rate of solar energy in agricultural field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red carbon dots
High quantum yield
Oxidation
Antioxidant sunlight conversion film
Practical agricultural planting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of RCDs/PVA film and antioxidant RCDs/PVA film.
Record the spectra of the light converted by the pure PVA film and the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film.
Cultivation of mung bean sprouts under the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant No. 21671070), the Project of GDUPS (2018) for Prof. Bingfu LEI, the National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFB0407902), and the Guangdong Provincial Special Fund for Modern Agriculture Industry Technology Innovation Teams (No. 2021KJ122).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of RCDs. Inset: particle distribution. (b-c) HRTEM image of RCDs. (d) SAED pattern of RCDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of RCDs. (b) XPS survey pattern of RCDs. (c-f) High-resolution C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and S 2p XPS spectra of RCDs.

(a) UV–vis spectrum of RCDs. (b) The excitation spectrum (green line) of RCDs monitored at 600 nm and the emission spectra of RCDs excited at 285 and 540 nm (red and purple line). (c) The fluorescence emission spectra of RCDs excited at different wavelength. (d) The fluorescence emission spectra of RCDs dispersed in different solvents.

(a) The FT-IR spectra of the original RCDs and the RCDs after irradiation for several days. (b) EPR result of 1O2 generated by TEMP (black line) and RCDs in the dark (blue line) and under the irradiation of xenon lamp (500 W, 10 min) (red line). (c) The fluorescence of RCDs(pH = 8.0), RCDs with NaBH4 (pH = 8.5) and RCDs with alkali (pH = 8.5). (d) The fluorescence of RCDs, KMnO4 treated RCDs and KMnO4 and NaBH4 treated RCDs.

(a) The excitation spectrum and the emission spectra of the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film. (b) The changes of fluorescence intensities of the RCDs/PVA film and the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film in three days. (c) The spectra recorded by fiber optical spectrometer as the light produced by Xenon lamp through PVA film and antioxidant RCDs/PVA film. Inset: the pictures of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film under the natural light and UV light. (d) The detail of Fig. 5c in red region.
The formation and oxidation-induced quenching mechanism the RCDs and the functioning principle of antioxidant sunlight conversion film.

(a) The growth of mung bean sprouts of the control group and experimental group. (b) The fresh weight and the dry weight of plants of the control group and experimental group. (c) The content of chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b of the control group and experimental group. The measurements were carried out in 8 replicates for each group. Marked with * (p < 0.05) and ** (p < 0.01) suggests a significant difference from the control. (CK: control group with pure PVA film; RCDs: experimental group with antioxidant RCDs/PVA film).

head.ce:title: Oxidation-induced quenching mechanism of ultrabright red carbon dots and application in antioxidant RCDs/PVA film

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) dominated by surface state emission mechanism were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method, the photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of RCDs reaches to 78.3% in N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF). RCDs mainly absorb UV and yellowish green light and emit red light in the region from 550 to 800 nm, which perfectly matches the light needed by plant for photosynthesis. Besides, the fluorescence quenching of RCDs is ascribed to oxidation on the surface of RCDs by oxygen. Further evidences about that are obtained from the changes in fluorescence, singlet oxygen (1O2) signal of RCDs and the amount of O elements between the RCDs and quenched RCDs (QCDs). Antioxidant RCDs/PVA film, served as an agricultural sunlight conversion film, consisting of RCDs, polyvinyl alcohol-1799 (PVA-1799) and ascorbic acid, not only converts yellowish green light (500–610 nm) to red light (610–800 nm) from RCDs with tough, environment-friendly, waterproof properties from PVA-1799, but also gain antioxidant ability on account of ascorbic acid preventing the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film from fluorescence quenching. When antioxidant RCDs/PVA film was applied to the practical agricultural planting, the fresh weight, dry weight and the chlorophyll a content of the mung bean sprouts increased by 10.4% and 13.9%, and 7.1%, respectively. The application of the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film improve the utilization rate of solar energy in agricultural field.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as prospective nanomaterials for widespread application, such as light conversion material [1], biomedicine [2], bioimaging [3], photosynthesis [4]. Compared to organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs have distinctive advantages of small size, low cytotoxicity, high quantum yield (QY), high photostability and so on [5,6]. To date, many works about blue and green CDs with ultrahigh QY that prepared by simple synthesis have been reported [7,8]. For example, Zhang et al. synthesized blue CDs with 99% QY through one-step microwave synthesis [7]. However, the investigation of the ultrabright long-wavelength emissive CDs from simple preparation methods remains difficult. Qu’s group successfully prepared long-wavelength emissive CDs from the same precursor [9–11]. Their group had been working on the preparation of long-wavelength emissive CDs with high QY for a long time, and they have successfully prepared orange CDs with QY of 46% [12]. Fan and his coworkers obtained red CDs with ultrahigh QY up to 86% through column chromatography by using a mixture of methylene chloride and methanol as the eluent [13]. Yang and his coworkers also devoted themselves to improving the QY of CDs, and the QY of the long-wavelength emissive (deep red) CDs reached to 59% [14]. Most of reported CDs did not have high QY from simple separation methods. Hence, it is of great significance to find strategies that make it easy to synthesize ultrabright red or NIR CDs for further application.

Besides, when the CDs are put in different environment, their fluorescence gradually decreases or nearly quenches. For example, CDs served as sensors of different metalions on account of the fluorescence of CDs often decreases with the increased concentration of metal ions [15]. The fluorescence changes origin from the changes of surface of the CDs [16]. The CDs also can be quenched by the high temperature when they are encapsulated for LED applications [17]. What’s more, the fluorescence of CDs is also related to the light irradiation according to our previous work [18]. However, the detailed reasons for the quenching of CDs under the light are rarely studied. One of the reasons why CDs-based LEDs have not been commercialized yet are that CDs are not photostable compared to the traditional phosphors. How to improve the photostability of the CDs under high intensity irradiation is important.

With the rapid increase of the world population and changes in human demand for food quality, greenhouse vegetable cultivation has attracted more and more attention. Various crops can be obtained in vegetable plastic greenhouse regardless of season and weather, and greenhouses can cultivate pollution-free vegetables with better vegetable quality. For greenhouse planting, the film covered in the greenhouse is of great importance. What’s more, the crops mainly absorb and utilize blue and red light. Red and NIR light is more significant to them while the ultra violet (UV), green and yellow are barely been utilized [19,20]. Hence, in order to take full advantage of the energy, light conversion film consisting of matrix (high-molecular polymer) and sunlight conversion agents should replace ordinary greenhouse film. Polyethylene is the traditional commonly used matrix, but it is harder to degrade in the soil. Besides, You et al. used carboxymethyl cellulose as the matrix, but it will dissolve in water [21]. As for sunlight conversion agents, many studies about rare-earth ion doped phosphors were reported. For example, Lian’s grouphas devoted themselves to obtaining phosphor to the agricultural film for a long time [22,23]. However, exploiting and utilizing rare earth element is not environmentally friendly, and the application of traditional phosphors will make the film lose permeability to a large extent because of the large size of the inorganic luminescent material. In recent year, with the development of CDs, some studies about CDs as light conversion agents were reported. For example, He et al. prepared Eu3+ and CDs co-doping composite as light conversion agent [24]. You et al. successfully prepared sunlight conversion film based on carboxymethyl cellulose and blue emissive CDs [21]. The reported CDs-based light conversion agents are related to short-wavelength emissive CDs rather than long-wavelength emissive CDs such as red and far-red CDs. What’s more, most reported CDs-based light conversion agents have not yet been applied to the practical agricultural planting.

In this work, red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) were successfully synthesized through one-step hydrothermal method. The RCDs can dissolve in different solvents with high QYs, such as the 34.7% QY in ethanol, 61.6% in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 78.3% in N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF). What’s more, RCDs absorb UV light and yellowish green light (500–610 nm) and emit red light (550–800 nm) while have less absorption in blue and red region, which perfectly matches the utilization of sunlight by plants. Through comparing the changes of fluorescence, singlet oxygen (1O2) signal of RCDs and the amount of oxygen elements between RCDs and quenched RCDs (QCDs) which are exposed to the sunlight for several days, the reason why fluorescence quenching happen to RCDs is that the surface of RCDs can be oxidized by oxygen. Besides, a special matrix, polyvinyl alcohol-1799 (PVA-1799), which only dissolves in hot water (90–95C), rather than in cold or warm water. Compared with polyethylene, PVA-1799 has higher compatibility and is more easily degraded by soil microorganisms [25]. In this study, the RCDs are dispersed in the PVA-1799 to form RCDs/PVA film. Up to now, there is no report on the application of red CDs in sunlight conversion film for practical agricultural planting. In order to improve the antioxidant ability of the agriculture film, ascorbic acid, a commonly used reducing agent, was added into the film to prevent the RCDs from being oxidized. The results showed that the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film (RCDs/PVA film doped with ascorbic acid) is more durable to sunlight irradiation than that of RCDs/PVA film. The antioxidant RCDs/PVA film can transform green-yellow light (500–610 nm) of the sunlight to the red light (610–800 nm). The proportion of red and far-red light of the spectrum that convert by the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film from sunlight increases while the blue region of sunlight remains nearly constant, which effectively enhances plant photosynthesis. The antioxidant RCDs/PVA film maintains CDs’ fluorescence properties, as well as the performances of toughness, environment friendly, resistance of water from PVA-1799, but also has excellent oxidation resistance. The antioxidant RCDs/PVA film are applied in the practical agricultural planting. Compared to the bare PVA film, the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film can promote the growth of mung bean sprouts. The average fresh weight, dry weight and chlorophyll content of the mung bean sprouts increase to a certain degree. This work is a breakthrough with great importance in the field of CD-based sunlight conversion film.

0.01 mol dopamine hydrochloride and 0.02 mol o-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water, and then added 2 mL 98% concentrated H2SO4 to adjust the pH of the solution reaching 0. The obtained solution was transferred into the 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 6 h. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 18000 rpm for 5 min to obtain black sediment after the reactor was cooled to room temperature. Next, the black sediment was washed with deionized water for 3 times to remove the impurities. Finally, the RCDs powder was obtained after being drying in a vacuum freeze-dryer. Another simple separation method to obtain RCDs is to directly dialyze the obtained solution in a 3500 Da dialysis bag for one day and then collect sediments which are RCDs aggregates from the dialysis bag. RCDs powder can be easily dispersed in the ethanol for further application.

RCDs solution (the solvent is ethanol) was contained in a beaker. Then the solution was exposed to sunlight and the air until the fluorescence of RCDs was completely quenched after 10 days. Finally, the treated solution was dried in an oven to obtain QCD powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of RCDs/PVA film and antioxidant RCDs/PVA film.
ce:section.ce:para: 2 mg RCDs powder was dispersed in 5 mL ethanol to obtain RCDs solution with red fluorescence. 0.5 g polyvinyl alcohol 1799 (PVA-1799) was added into the deionized water, and then transferred the mixture to the oil bath pan at 95 °C to dissolve the PVA-1799. Stirred and mixed different amount of RCDs solution and as-prepared PVA-1799 solution evenly to obtain RCDs/PVA solution (the mass of the RCDs powder: 100, 200, 300, 400 μg; the mass of PVA: 0.5 g). RCDs/PVA solution wasnaturally dried in a dry atmosphere, and the RCDs/PVA film was formed. The preparation method of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film is similar to that of RCDs/PVA film, but additionally added 50 μg ascorbic acid into the mixture during the preparation of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Record the spectra of the light converted by the pure PVA film and the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film.
ce:section.ce:para: The light was produced by Xenon lamp mimics sunlight. The as-prepared pure PVA film and the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film were put in front of the integrating sphere under the irradiation of the Xenon lamp. The light intensity of light resource remained unchanged, and then the fiber optic spectrometer was used to capture the light converted by pure PVA film and the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cultivation of mung bean sprouts under the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film.
ce:section.ce:para: The mung bean sprout seeds were cultured in aqueous solution for a few days. Bean sprouts with consistent growth were selected and then placed into different culture tubes under a light source consisting of blue, green and red light. The bean sprouts were divided into two group with 20 seedlings per group. Cultured two different groups under the pure PVA film and antioxidant RCDs/PVA film for 12 days, respectively.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded with an F-700 Hitachi fluorescence spectrofluorometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a JASCO v-570 spectrofluorometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) were carried out with Brooker (Vertex 70) Fourier infrared spectrometer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and the derivative thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) were measured by thermal gravimetric analyzer (TG209F1LibraTM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) patterns were obtained by X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (Thermo ESCALAB 250XI). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were performed on a JEOL JEM-2100F electron microscope. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded by a Zeiss scanning electron microscope (EVO MA 15). The decay lifetimes and quantum yield (QY) were measured by Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrofluorometer. Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) measurement were carried out by Bruker EMXplus electron paramagnetic resonance apparatus.

Herein, red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) were prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis from dopamine hydrochloride and o-phenylenediamine and obtained through simple centrifugation or dialysis procedures. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image reveals the diameter of 1.5 ~ 4.0 nm (average size ≈ 2.5 nm) for the RCDs. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows obvious lattices fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm that is consistent with the (100) diffraction facets of graphitic in Fig. 1b and 1c [26]. Besides, the lattice spacing calculated through the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) in Fig. 1d is also 0.21 nm.

To unveil the chemical compositions of RCDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) pattern were collected from the RCDs powder. In Fig. 2 a, the intense absorption band at 3200–3500 cm−1 FTIR spectrum correspond to the stretching vibration of OH and NH. The peaks at 2925, 1616, 1580, 1526 and 1470 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of aliphatic CH, aromatic CC, CN, NO2 and the bending vibration of NH, respectively. The peaks at 1319 and 1215 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of aromatic CN and CO, respectively. And the peaks at 758 and 617 cm−1 are the characteristic peaks of the CH out-of-plane bending vibration. The peaks at 1110 and 1050 cm−1 together determine the presence of C-SO2 and C-SO4 [27–30]. Further detection was carried out through the XPS analysis. The XPS survey pattern (Fig. 2b) indicates that RCDs mainly consist of C, N, O, S elements, and the atomic ratios are 75.3%, 12.7%, 10.4%, 1.6%, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2c, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum shows four peaks at 284.7, 285.8, 286.7, 288.0 eV, which are assigned for CC/CC, CN/CS, CO and CN, respectively. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.7, 400.2, and 402.3 eV, ascribed to pyridine N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N, respectively. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) was resolved into two peaks located at the binding energies of 531.5 and 533.0 eV, which are attributed to NO2 and COH groups. Besides, the high-resolution S2p spectrum (Fig. 2f) contains two peaks at 168.4 and 169.7 eV assigned for CSO2 C and CSO4 [31,32].

The UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 3 a) of RCDs solution exhibits two major absorption bands at the range of 250–300 nm and 460–600 nm, which are assigned to the π → π* transition of aromatic CC and n → π* transition of CN, respectively [33,34]. The optical properties of the RCDs are discussed next. As shown in the inset of Fig. 3b, under the irradiation of UV lamp, the RCDs solution emits red fluorescence. The excitation spectrum reveals two main band at 250–300 nm and 450–600 nm. The excitation bands are consistent with the major UV–vis absorption bands, which indicates that the absorptions may be related to the same kind of transitions that lead to emission, such as CN bonds [35,36]. From Fig. 3b, the fluorescence spectra of RCDs show that the optimal excitation is 258 nm while the optimal emission is 600 nm. The fluorescence emission spectrum has multiple peaks at 600, 650, 715 nm excited at 285 nm. Besides, some previous reported CDs also have asymmetric multiple peaks emission [37,38]. As shown in Fig. 3c, RCDs exhibit obvious excitation-independent photoluminescence, which can be attributed to their surface state emission [32]. The quantum yield (QY) of RCDs (dissolved in ethanol) was measured to be 34.7%. The RCDs powder also can be dispersed in different organic solvents, such as N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), acetone, formamide and glycol and so on. However, RCDs can’t dissolve in water. As shown in Fig. S1, RCDs powder settled on the bottom of the cuvette. Under the irradiation of UV lamp, the solution containing RCDs and water does not fluoresce. The fluorescence emission spectra of RCDs in different solvents are showed in Fig. 3d, and the shapes of the fluorescence emission peaks are the same while the locations of the peaks are different. According to the data from Table S1, except for ethanol, as viscosity of acetone, DMF, DMSO, formamide and glycol gradually increased, the emission peaks gradually appears redshift, which is similar to the previous report [39]. Besides, the fluorescence of CDs is affected by the hydrogen bonding [40]. When the RCDs were dissolved in ethanol, hydrogen bonds can be formed between hydroxyl of solvent and oxygen/nitrogen of RCDs, which makes emission peaks of the RCDs show bathochromic shift [41,42]. Formamide and glycol also can form hydrogen bonds with RCDs. The emission peaks of RCDs dissolved in solvents (formamide and glycol) are red-shifted than the emission peaks of RCDs dissolved in acetone, DMF, DMSO. Hence, the solvation effect of RCDs is related to the viscosity of the solvents and hydrogen bonding between RCDs and solvents. Besides, it is worth mentioning that when RCDs disperse in the DMSO or DMF, the QYs (the calculation method of QY is mentioned in Supporting Information) are higher, the QY of RCDs in DMSO is 61.6% while in DMF can reach to 78.3%, which is higher than most reported CDs. But the photostability of RCDs in DMF and DMOS is lower than that of in ethanol, which is not conducive to the preservation and application. Hence, RCDs were dissolved in ethanol and was used for further application. Besides, the decay curves (Fig. S2) of RCDs dispersed in different solvent (DMF, DMSO, acetone, formamide, ethanol and glycol) are fitted with a single-exponential function and the corresponding lifetime is 3.32, 3.17, 3.08, 2.44, 2.32, 2.09 ns, respectively, which demonstrates the emission peaks (600, 650, 715 nm) of RCDs origin from single emitting center. Besides, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and the derivative thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) (Fig. S3) were used to characterize the chemical Structure of RCDs. The curve shows that there are three main degradation process in RCDs. The first degradation process (from 30 to 110 °C) is the evaporation of the bonded water in the RCDs, which is corresponding to the broad absorption band at 3200–3500 cm−1 of the FT-IR spectrum. The second degradation step (260–370 °C) is attribute to changes of the functional groups (i.e. amino, hydroxy, ether bond, unsaturated double bond) on the surface of RCDs. The mass of the RCDs drops sharply in the third degradation process (370–500 °C), which ascribes to the pyrolysis of the core structure [24]. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of untreated RCDs and RCDs after been heating at different temperature (including 200 °C and 280 °C) for 1 h are showed in Fig. S4, and the inset of Fig. S4 is the luminescence of RCDs in these three states (from left to right) under the UV light excitation. TGA curve shows that the chemical structure of RCDs remains constant at 200 °C, but changes at 280 °C. The fluorescence intensity of the RCDs which is treated at 200 °C is closed to the untreated RCDs, while fluorescence intensity of the material treated at 280 °C dramatically declines because of the degradation of the surface functional groups of the RCDs. This phenomenon suggests that RCDs are dominated by a surface state emission mechanism.

Furthermore, the fluorescence of RCDs is different in various pH solutions (Fig. S5). The solution of RCDs dispersed in ethanol is alkaline. When the pH is 2, in the fluorescence spectrum, peaks at 600 nm disappears, while the integral fluorescence intensity gradually increases from pH 2 to 8. When the pH is higher than 8, the fluorescence intensity of RCDs solution decreases. These properties indicate that the RCDs are dominated by surface state, which was revealed by previous work [43,44]. To further investigate the surface state emission mechanism of the RCDs, the other two materials were prepared by changing the reaction temperature. The RCDs-150 and RCDs-250 were synthesized through one-step hydrothermal process under the temperature of 150 °C and 250 °C, respectively, and other reaction conditions remain invariable. With the reaction temperature going up, synthesis yield of nanoparticles increases. The synthesis yield of RCD is about 29%, while the synthesis yield of RCDs-150 is 0.25% and RCDs-250 is 38%. According to the TEM images (Fig. S6a), the degree of carbonization of RCDs-150 is lower, and they do not appear to be a regular sphere. What’s more, they are not resistant to high voltage strikes, so they will disappear under the high-resolution condition. The average size of 250-RCDs is 3.9 nm, which is larger than those of RCD according to Fig. S6b. In addition, the QY of RCDs-150 and RCDs-250 is 22.5% and 23.5%, respectively. With the increase of temperature, the surface functional groups of nanoparticles are gradually carbonized as a part of the core of the CDs, which leads to the decrease of QY of nanomaterials. This result further proves the surface state emission mechanism of RCDs [45].

It is worth mentioning that the RCDs also have the characteristics of upconversion luminescence. As shown in Fig. S7, RCDs solution can be excited by NIR photons, and the optimal emission of RCDs is 650 nm with two shoulder peaks at 600 and 710 nm, which is similar to one-photon fluorescence. The photograph of RCDs solution under the NIR light irradiation was showed inset Fig. S7. The as-prepared RCDs can be applied in the bioimaging because of their up-conversion fluorescence properties. Red and NIR light have strong penetrability to cells and can well avoid the spontaneous fluorescence.

Based on the excellent optical properties that RCDs absorb UV and yellowish green light and the emit red light with high QY, RCDs have great potential for the practical agricultural planting application to achieve enhancement of crops photosynthesis. Our previous reports manifest that red and far-red light can be efficiently absorbed and applied by crops [16]. The chloroplasts of plant mainly utilize blue and red regions of the sunlight and too much UV light can cause a stress response in plants. Furthermore, green and yellow light has little effect on plant growth. The as-prepared RCDs absorb UV and yellowish green light which are difficult to be used by plant and emit red light that can be highly taken advantage of for photosynthesis. Hence, RCDs can be applied in the field of agricultural sunlight conversion film.

RCDs were doped into PVA-1799 to form a light conversion film (RCDs/PVA film). PVA-1799 is a special high-molecular polymer and has excellent transmittance and toughness, eco-friendly, water resistance, and rapid degradation capacity. It can be a perfect matrix to form a light conversion film on the practical agricultural planting. The fluorescence spectra RCDs/PVA film is showed in Fig. S8. Dispersing in the PVA matrix does not change the fluorescence properties of the RCDs, and the fluorescence of RCDs/PVA film are the same as that of RCDs. Doping RCDs into PVA makes the RCDs have better photostability compared to the RCDs dissolved in the solvent. However, the fluorescence of RCDs solution and RCDs/PVA film has a certain extent decrease under the solar radiation. As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence of RCDs/PVA film decreased by 80% after three days under the solar radiation. Those are essentially caused by the quenching of RCDs in different circumstances. As a light conversion film on agriculture, fluorescence stability is the fundamental parameters. It is of great importance to improve the photostability of CDs. To explore the reasons of fluorescence quenching, characterizations and measurements were carried out.

The red fluorescence of RCDs gradually quenched after exposed to the sunlight for several days, which is similar to the previous reports [18,46]. The obtained materials were as QCDs. As shown in Fig. S10, the average size of QCDs is 3.5 nm, and the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, which is as the same as the RCDs, indicating that the surface of RCDs changed but the core remains unchanged. In Fig. 4 a, compared with RCDs, the FT-IR spectrum of QCDs shows that, the peak at 1696 cm−1 corresponding to the CO appears while the peak at 1580 cm−1 which is ascribed to CN bond disappears. The functional groups on the surface of RCDs have changed, and the disappearance of CN is accompanied by fluorescence quenching which can conclude that the emission of RCDs is related to the CN. The Raman spectra of RCDs and QCDs are showed in Fig. S11. The Raman spectrum of RCDs (Fig. S11a) has two obvious peaks at 1360 and 1471 cm−1, attributed to D band and G band. The peaks at 610, 1150 and 1230 cm−1 ascribe to the CC, CH and COH bond, respectively [47,48]. The Raman spectrum of QCDs has two intense and broad peaks at 1350 and 1560 cm−1, ascribed to the D band and G band. The peak at 650 cm−1 is assigned to CC bond. But the peaks related to CH and COH disappear. What’s more, Due to solar irradiation, the surface functional groups of RCDs have changed in the air. The full width at half maximum of D and G band of RCDs and QCDs are different because of and the crystallization degree and the morphology of them are different. The intensity ratio ID/IG is found to decrease from 1.54 to 0.96 for RCDs and QCDs, indicating that RCDs have more amorphous structures, while the graphitization degree of QCDs strengthens. Besides, compared to RCDs, there are not many peaks in the Raman spectrum of QCDs. The reason is that the contents of surface functional groups of QCDs decrease, resulting in the vibration of the relevant functional groups is too weak to be detected. The result shows that the chemical groups between RCDs and QCDs are different, and the amount of the chemical groups between them is different. Besides, more insights into the RCDs and QCDs can be obtained from XPS (Fig. S12&S13). The atomic ratios (C, N, O, S elements) of QCDs are 75.6%, 9.9%, 13.7%, 0.8%, respectively, according to the XPS spectrum (Fig. S12). Compared to the RCDs, the content of O increases, but the content of N decreases, which is consistent with analysis of FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. S13a-d, the high-resolution N 1s and S 2p spectra of QCDs are similar with the those of RCDs, While the high-resolution C 1s and O 1s spectra are different from the result of RCDs. The high-resolution spectra of QCDs show thatCO and CO bond appear while CN disappears after they are exposed to the sunlight. All the datas indicate that, after further irradiation of sunlight, the surface states, and mainly the oxygen-related surface states, on the carbon dots increased. Combined the XPS and FT-IR spectra, it is preliminarily concluded that the oxidation reaction occurred at the RCDs, and then QCDs were obtained after the illumination. Furthermore, a more intuitive characterization which demonstrates the oxidation of the RCDs is presented in electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) Measurements. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) include ·O2 −, ·OH, 1O2 and so on. We detected the signal of ·O2 −, ·OH, 1O2 through EPR measurements. As a result, only the signal of 1O2 could be detected. 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) is served as a trapping reagent to capture the singlet oxygen (1O2). When TEMP is added to solution, TEMP captures 1O2 to form TEMPO. When there is the signal of TEMPO, it means the presence of 1O2. In Fig. 4b, the EPR spectra show that the RCDs have singlet oxygen (1O2) signal in the light but not in the dark. The presence of 1O2 manifests the existence of oxidation reaction [49]. However, when the RCDs solution is put in the air and in an atmosphere of argon, the signal strength of 1O2 is different. The 1O2 signal of RCDs solution in the air is stronger than that of in the argon, indicating that the presence of oxygen can accelerate the oxidation reaction of RCDs solution under the light irradiation. More intuitively, the fluorescence changes of RCDs treated by the air and argon (the solution was put in the closed container) under the light irradiation are different. As shown in Fig. S14, the fluorescence of RCDs solution in the air sharply decreased, but only slightly in argon. This phenomenon further demonstrates that the fluorescence quenching of RCDs is related to oxidation on the surface of RCDs by oxygen. Besides, another visible method to prove that it is the oxidation that cause fluorescence quenching of RCDs is the change of CDs’ fluorescence. NaBH4 has strong selective reducibility that can selectively reduce the carbonyl group and aldehyde group to hydroxyl group, and can also reduce the carboxyl group to aldehyde group [50]. The fluorescence spectrum of the RCDs placed in the air for a period of time with the pH = 8.0 is shown in the Fig. 4c (black line). When NaBH4 was added into the oxidized RCDs ethanol solution (the pH is 8.5), the fluorescence intensity (red line) increased obviously, while those of the RCDs without NaBH4 but the pH is as same as the former decline (the pH is 8.5). In order to simulate the redox process of RCDs in the natural environment and under the treatment of NaBH4, KMnO4 was used as oxidant. According to Fig. 4d, as the 0.5 mg KMnO4 added into the RCDs solution (0.4 mg/L), the fluorescence sharply deceased. Subsequently, the addition of 10 mg NaBH4 into the solution that was oxidized by KMnO4 made the fluorescence of the solution increases. As shown in Fig. S15, in the presence of oxidant and reductant, the fluorescence changes of RCDs are macroscopic. This phenomenon further certifies that when the RCDs are oxidized, the fluorescence of them are gradually quenched; but when the reducing agent is used in the oxidized RCDs, RCDs are reduced and the fluorescence of the them is recovered to some extent. Besides, ascorbic acid, with antioxidant property, can serve as the inhibitor to remove the signal of 1O2. As shown in Fig. S16, the 1O2 signal disappears when ascorbic acid is added in to the RCDs solution. Here, ascorbic acid was added into the RCDs to further demonstrate the oxidation-inducedquenching mechanism of RCDs. In Fig. S17a, the fluorescence of the solution containing RCDs and ascorbic acid barely decreases in the first 3 h under the irradiation of xenon lamp (light intensity: 20,000 lx), and the fluorescence of this solution decreases slowly after 3 h. As shown in Fig. S17b, the fluorescence RCDs solution without ascorbic acid decrease significantly from the beginning. This phenomenon reveals that ascorbic acid can prevent the fluorescence quenching of RCDs. All above measurements and the corresponding results show that the fluorescence quenching of the RCDs is related to the oxidation of the surface of carbon dots under the light.

Fluorescence quenching of RCDs occurs because of the oxidation reaction, but ascorbic acid can protect RCDs from being oxidized. Hence, during preparing the light conversion film, ascorbic acid was added into the film. The light conversion film containing ascorbic acid are named antioxidant RCDs/PVA film. RCDs powder were dissolved into the ethanol, and then added the as-prepared RCDs solution and 50 μg ascorbic acid into the PVA-1799 solution to form the antioxidant agricultural sunlight film after natural withering. SEM images (Fig. S18a, S18b) of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film show that the surface of film is relatively smooth, and the average thickness is approximately 50 μm. Besides, the mapping (Fig. S18c-S18h) of the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film shows that the C、N、O and S elements are evenly distributed in the film indicating the uniform distribution of the RCDs in the PVA film. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence spectra of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film are similar to those of RCDs/PVA film (Fig. S8), indicating that the fluorescent characteristics origin from the RCDs. Compared to the RCDs/PVA film, according to the UV–vis spectrum of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film (Fig. S19), the absorption in UV region significantly enhances, but the overall trend remains unchanged. Besides, as shown in Fig. S20, the film with ascorbic acid is transparent and does not fluoresce, indicating that ascorbic acid does not change the transmittance and fluorescence properties of the PVA film. The addition of ascorbic acid improves the oxidation resistance property of the light conversion film through reducing the degree of fluorescence quenching of RCDs. As shown in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensity of the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film decreased by 15% while the RCDs/PVA film decreased by 55% under the sun exposure for 3 days. The result reveals that the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film is more resistant to oxidation, because ascorbic acid as a sacrificial agent is more easily oxidized during the oxidation process, thus preventing the oxidation of doping RCDs. The existence of ascorbic acid of the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film not only maintain the performance of the light conversion film, but also effectively prevent the carbon dots from being oxidized.

When different amounts of RCDs (100, 200, 300, 400 μg) doping into the PVA-1799, as shown in Fig. S21a, with the increase of the mass of RCDs, the fluorescence intensities gradually increase. However, the transmittances of the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film has decreased in the same time in Fig. S21b. The permeability of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film in the blue and red region is relatively high and main absorb UV and yellowish green light, testifying the weak absorption of blue and red light. This performance is the most basic characteristic of a material as a light conversion film. These two properties commonly decide the conversion of the sunlight. Subsequently, the optimal conversion effect of the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film were recorded by fiber optical spectrometer compared to the pure PVA film in Fig. 5c when 200 μg RCDs are added. In the same time, the QY of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film is 21.57%. Besides, the decay curve (Fig. S22) of the film can be fitted with single-exponential function. The lifetime of the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film is calculated to be 3.27 ns. The decay curve of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film is close to of RCDs, further demonstrating the fluorescence properties of film origin from RCDs. The detail of Fig. 5c at the range of red region is showed in Fig. 5d. From Fig. 5c and 5d, the light intensity decreases in green-yellow region (500–610 nm) and increases in red (610–800 nm) through the transformation of the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film. Hence, it could strengthen the intensity mainly in red light range (610–800 nm) compared to the pure PVA film. However, as shown in Fig. S23, the intensity of UV region (250–300 nm) of sunlight is very weak. Hence, the light conversion effect of the light conversion film isinsignificant. It means that the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film can transform yellowish green to red light while the blue light almost remains relatively constant. According to the UV–vis absorption spectrum of chloroplasts of the crops (Fig. S24, green line), chloroplasts mainly absorb blue (400–500 nm) and red light (630–700 nm), which can perfectly match the spectrum of sunlight after transforming by the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film. Hence, the RCDs can serve as light conversion agents in the agriculture field.

Next, the chemical and physical properties of the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film are further discussed. The PVA-1799 is a special material that is a one-solution-type material. It is almost no longer soluble in water after it dried out for the first time following the heat dissolution. As shown in Fig. S25, with the extension of soaking time in the water, the fluorescence of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film drops to a very small extent. The fluorescence intensity of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film decreased by 10% in 6 days. What’s more, antioxidant RCDs/PVA film also has good heat resistance. As shown in Fig. S26, the fluorescence intensity decreased by 14% in 6 days at 70 ℃. What’s more, according to tensile curve (Fig. S27), the maximum elongation of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film is longer and tension borne is larger than those of the pure PVA film. Lots of functional groups on the surface of the RCDs enhances the tensile property of the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film. To sun up, the as-prepared antioxidant RCDs/PVA film is beneficial to be agricultural light conversion film for its excellent physical and chemical properties.

The formation and oxidation-induced quenching mechanism of the RCDs and the functioning principle of antioxidant sunlight conversion film are showed in Fig. 6 . Dopamine hydrochloride and o-phenylenediamine were added into an acidic aqueous solution at 200 ℃ for 6 h after ultrasonication for 10 min. During the hydrothermal reaction process, individual hydrophobic RCD dominated by a surface state emission mechanism was formed first, and then those scattered nanoparticles were aggregated and the fluoresence of them were quenched in water. When the mass of the accumulated RCDs reached a certain level, the RCDs clusters precipitated to the bottom of the reactor in the form of gravity settling. The RCDs were obtained by simple separation methods including dialysis or centrifugation. The surface of RCDs will be quenched by the O2 which is demonstrated by different characterizations. Next, the solution of RCDs dispersed in the ethanol were doped into the PVA-1799 solution, and the antioxidants which prevent RCDs from being oxidized were added in the same time. The ascorbic acid served as the antioxidants for its more sensitive to oxidizing agents than RCDs. Ascorbic acid improved the oxidation resistance of the light conversion film without changing the optical properties of the film. Hence, the as-prepared antioxidant RCDs/PVA film can better resist the oxidation of oxygen and are more durable to sunlight for its further applications. Ultimately, an antioxidant RCDs/PVA film is applied to an agricultural light conversion film because of its property to convert yellowish green light to red light without changing the transmittance of blue and red regions of the sunlight. Under the action of the film, the growth of crops will be promoted.

Based on the sunlight conversion performance of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film, it was used as the agricultural sunlight conversion film for the mung bean seedlings, and the pure PVA film as the control. Two groups (experimental group and control group) of seedlings were cultured for 12 days under the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film and pure PVA film, respectively. The effect of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film on the growth and physical signs of plants were investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the growth of mung bean seedlings reveals that the average plant height of the plants under the influence of antioxidant RCDs/PVA film is higher than that of the control. Besides, compared to the control group, the average of fresh weight and dry weight of the experimental group increase in a degree. From Fig. 7b, the fresh weight of the plants increases by 10.4% and the dry weight increases by 13.9%. Through the transformation of light by antioxidant RCDs/PVA film, the proportion of red and far-red light increased, which is more conducive to plant photosynthesis because of Emerson effect [16]. Hence, the biomass of the plants is promoted under the light transformation agricultural film. In the same time, the red and far-red light are good for the accumulation of chlorophyll. In Fig. 7c, the average content of chlorophyll a of the experimental group has significant difference to the control group, and increases by 7.1%. The average content of chlorophyll b of treatment group is higher than that of control group. Those results demonstrate that the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film served as the light conversion agricultural film can promote the growth of plants. In other words, the film improves the efficiency of solar energy.

RCDs with high QY were successfully prepared by one-step hydrothermal method from the dopamine hydrochloride and o-phenylenediamine. The as-prepared RCDs can be easily obtained by the separation means of simple centrifugation or dialysis. The RCDs mainly absorb UV and yellowish green light and emit red light and have upconversion luminescence characteristic. RCDs with ultrabright luminescence are demonstrated that they are dominated by surface state emission mechanism. The results of FT-IR, XPS and fluorescence spectra between RCDs and QCDs and the EPR of RCDs reveal that fluorescence quenching of RCDs are due to their surface being oxidized by oxygen. The antioxidant RCDs/PVA film with great properties including hydrophobicity, environmentally friendly, resistance of tensile and antioxidant ability was successfully constructed and realizes that convert yellowish green light to red light. Compared to the pure PVA film, the growth of plant shows that the presence of the antioxidant RCDs/PVA film enhanced the photosynthesis in practical agricultural planting, and it shows in the increase of the contents of biomass and chlorophyll. Red or NIR CDs served as light conversion agents to form antioxidant agricultural sunlight conversion film is with great potential in the cultivation of greenhouse vegetable.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant No. 21671070), the Project of GDUPS (2018) for Prof. Bingfu LEI, the National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFB0407902), and the Guangdong Provincial Special Fund for Modern Agriculture Industry Technology Innovation Teams (No. 2021KJ122).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.131653.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red emission B, N, S-codoped carbon dots for colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode detection of Fe3+ ions in complex biological fluids and living cells

Chen, Red emission B, N, S-codoped carbon dots for colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode detection of Fe3+ ions in complex biological fluids and living cells, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 9 (2017) 12663-12672.
